[YEAR-001-013]Mr. Smith continues his round and enters the reporters' hall. Here 1500 reporters, in their respective places, facing an equal number of telephones, are communicating to the subscribers the news of the world as gathered during the night. The organization of this matchless service has often been described. Besides his telephone, each reporter, as the reader is aware, has in front of him a set of commutators, which enable him to communicate with any desired telephotic line. Thus the subscribers not only hear the news but see the occurrences. When an incident is described that is already past, photographs of its main features are transmitted with the narrative. And there is no confusion withal. The reporters' items, just like the different stories and all the other component parts of the journal, are classified automatically according to an ingenious system, and reach the hearer in due succession. Furthermore, the hearers are free to listen only to what specially concerns them. They may at pleasure give attention to one editor and refuse it to another.

Aouda is the young Parsee woman whom [[phileas-fogg|Phileas Fogg]] rescues from death during his passage through India, and who becomes his companion for the remainder of the voyage. The narrative gives her history plainly — her name was Aouda, and, left an orphan, she was married against her will (AWED-013-011) — establishing her as both victim and, ultimately, Fogg's equal in feeling.

The Tabor Island Castaway is the discovery, in *[[the-mysterious-island|The Mysterious Island]]*, of a human being whom long solitude has stripped of reason.
